title: Osso buco
subtitle: Milanese braised shin, slow-cooked with wine, stock and gremolata.
excerpt: Cross-cut bone-in beef shin braised in white wine, stock and vegetables until the meat falls from the bone and the marrow slides from the cavity. Finished with gremolata. Traditionally served with risotto alla milanese. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Osso buco alla milanese. The dish is documented in Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1929) and in numerous mid-20th-century Italian cookbooks. Traditionally made with veal shin; beef shin is used here as a widely available substitute with comparable technique.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the braised shin in its sauce for up to two months. Reheat gently on the hob.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made the day before. Reheat covered in a low oven at 160°C for 30 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A garnish of finely chopped lemon zest, garlic and flat-leaf parsley. Stirred into or scattered over braised dishes at the end; the fresh citrus cuts through rich, long-cooked sauces.

content.text: Osso buco means 'bone with a hole' — the cross-cut shin with its central marrow cavity. The marrow melts during the braise and enriches the sauce; eating it from the bone with a small spoon is part of the ritual. The gremolata served at the end is not decoration: the fresh lemon zest and garlic cut through the richness in a way that transforms the dish.
Ask a butcher to cut the shin into 4 cm cross-sections. Tie each piece with kitchen twine to stop the meat falling away from the bone during the long braise.

items.prepNote: cross-cut, bone-in, about 4 cm thick, tied with twine

content.text: Pat {{beef-shin}} shin pieces dry with kitchen paper. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper, then dust lightly with {{plain-flour}} of flour. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the shin pieces for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deeply golden. Work in batches; don't crowd the pan. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pan, cook {{onion}} diced onion, {{celery}} diced celery and {{carrot}} diced carrot for 10 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic; cook for 2 minutes. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and let it reduce by half. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes, {{stock-chicken}} of stock, {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. Return the shin pieces to the pot. The liquid should come about halfway up the meat.

Cover and cook on the lowest possible heat for 1.5 to 2 hours, turning the meat once, until it is very tender and beginning to part from the bone. Mix the gremolata: combine lemon zest, finely grated garlic and chopped parsley. Scatter over the dish at the table or stir in just before serving. Remove the twine and bay leaves.

items.symptom: Meat falling off the bone before it's cooked
items.cause: Twine not tied tightly enough, or pieces not thick enough.
items.fix: Make sure twine is secure before browning. Thinner pieces cook faster but fall apart; use 4 cm minimum.

items.cause: Too much liquid, or lid not slightly ajar.
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 20 minutes to let the sauce reduce. Alternatively, remove the meat, boil the sauce down, and return.

items.cause: Too much raw garlic, or added too early.
items.fix: Add gremolata at the very end or at the table. A little goes a long way; start with half and taste.

content.text: Osso buco belongs to the serious winter table in Milan. It is traditionally served with saffron risotto, the two dishes completing each other: the rich, gelatinous braise alongside the golden, creamy rice. The marrow, eaten from the bone cavity with a small spoon, is considered the cook's reward.
